query_results:
Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: A Year-Long Surveillance (2023) at the Largest Infectious Disease ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11672838/

SUMMARY:
The quarterly analysis of resistance patterns highlights distinct trends, with the highest levels observed in Quarter 3, particularly in Acinetobacter (88.24%) and Pseudomonas (22.02%) . This alarming rise underscores an urgent need for intensified infection control and antimicrobial stewardship measures.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Global trends in antibiotic consumption during 2016–2023 ... - PNAS ---
URL: https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2411919121

SUMMARY:
We find that estimated antibiotic consumption in reported countries increased 16.3% from 29.5 to 34.3 billion defined daily doses (DDDs) from 2016 to 2023.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: Antimicrobial resistance - World Health Organization (WHO) ---
URL: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/antimicrobial-resistance

SUMMARY:
*   [Health Topics](https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/antimicrobial-resistance#) *   **Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the top global public health and development threats. To address AMR in a programmatic manner that puts people, their needs and equitable access to health services at the centre of the AMR response in the community, in primary care, secondary and tertiary care, and at national and/or subnational level, WHO has developed the People-centred approach to addressing antimicrobial resistance in human health and WHO core package of interventions to support national action plans. To help fill the major gaps in R&D for antimicrobials, vaccines and diagnostic tools, WHO works closely with organizations such as the Global Antibiotic Research & Development Partnership (GARDP), the AMR Action Fund, and the Combating Antibiotic Resistant Bacteria Biopharmaceutical Accelerator (CARB-X).

FULL CONTENT:
Antimicrobial resistance 

===============

# Antimicrobial Resistance Facts and Stats

## Key points

![AR Publications and Reports shows images of various reports reflected on the page.](/antimicrobial-resistance/media/images/AR-Publications-thumb.jpg "AR Publications and Reports Thumbnail")

## Overview

CDC is concerned about the emergence and spread of new forms of resistance and rising resistant infections in the community (outside hospitals). Community infections can put more people at risk, make spread more difficult to identify and contain and threaten the progress made to protect patients in hospitals.

In 2019, CDC's *[Antibiotic Resistance Threats in the United States, 2019](/antimicrobial-resistance/media/pdfs/2019-ar-threats-report-508.pdf)* (AR Threats Report) noted that dedicated prevention and infection control efforts in the U.S. reduced deaths from antimicrobial-resistant infections by 18% overall and by nearly 30% in hospitals. Additional data shows these reductions continued until 2020 when the U.S. started losing progress combating AR due to effects of the COVID-19 pandemic. The pandemic resulted in more resistant infections, increased antibiotic use, and less data and prevention actions.[1](#cdcreference_1)

In July 2024, CDC published [Antimicrobial Resistance Threats in the United States, 2021-2022](/antimicrobial-resistance/data-research/threats/update-2022.html), highlighting that new data[A](#cdcfootnote_1) show that six bacterial antimicrobial-resistant hospital-onset infections[B](#cdcfootnote_2) increased by a combined 20% during the COVID-19 pandemic compared to the pre-pandemic period, peaking in 2021, and remaining above pre-pandemic levels in 2022. In addition, the number of reported clinical cases of *Candida auris* (*C. auris*) - a type of yeast that can spread in healthcare facilities, is often resistant to antifungal medications, and can cause severe illness - increased nearly five-fold from 2019 to 2022.

## Highlights

## Tracking AR

CDC uses several data sources and systems to track AR in the U.S. and abroad. Knowing where and how changes in resistance are occurring informs solutions (e.g., outbreak response) to prevent spread and slow resistance.

CDC collects data through many tracking systems and uses external sources, like electronic health data, to assess the burden of antimicrobial-resistant germs. Data often include information on human infections, pathogens (germs) and reasons or risk factors that cause some people to get a resistant infection. CDC also works with partners to strengthen information-sharing, address data gaps and harmonize how data are reported across the U.S. and other countries.

The following systems complement each other to provide a comprehensive understanding of known and emerging AR threats.

![AR Lab Network logo. A map of the U.S. with gradient colors of teal, blue, purple, dark red, red and orange-red.](/antimicrobial-resistance-laboratory-networks/media/images/ARLN_Logo_24.jpg)
![EIP logo](/emerging-infections-program/media/images/eip-logo.jpg)
![High-tech globe in colors of blue, purple, and pink.](/antimicrobial-resistance-laboratory-networks/media/images/GARLRN_Banner.jpg)
![National Healthcare Safety Network (NHSN)](/nhsn/images/buttons/nhsn-logo.jpg)

DESCRIPTION: The graph illustrates the projected growth of the antimicrobial resistance diagnostics market size from 2018 to 2028, highlighting contributions from hospitals, diagnostic laboratories, and pharmaceutical sectors.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://www.grandviewresearch.com/static/img/research/us-antibiotic-resistance-market.png
DESCRIPTION: A bar chart illustrates the projected growth of the U.S. antibiotic resistance market from 2020 to 2030, highlighting various diseases with specific color-coded segments and indicating a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 4.3%.

IMAGE 3:
URL: http://cdn.statcdn.com/Infographic/images/normal/3095.jpeg
DESCRIPTION: A bar graph illustrates the projected increase in mortality from antimicrobial-resistant infections (AMR) from 1.3 million in 2020 to 8.7 million by 2050, while comparing it to other common causes of death, such as cancer and diabetes.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.clearvuehealth.com/images/antibiotic-resistance-research-statistics.001.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The graphic illustrates a declining trend in new antibiotic approvals across different time periods, highlighting 19 approvals from 1980-84 compared to only 6 from 2010-14, juxtaposed against the rising challenge of antibiotic resistance.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.clearvuehealth.com/images/antibiotic-resistance-research-statistics.002.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The graph illustrates the sharp increase in the identification of antibiotic resistance enzymes, specifically β-lactamase, from 1970 to 2000, highlighting the rapid evolution of bacteria to resist and destroy antibiotics.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Surveillance data show alarming resistance levels, especially in Acinetobacter (88.24%) and Pseudomonas (22.02%), signaling urgent need for enhanced infection control measures.
Existing treatments are increasingly ineffective due to rising antibiotic consumption (16.3% increase from 2016 to 2023), highlighting the inadequacy of current therapeutic strategies.
The WHO emphasizes the urgent requirement for innovative research and development in antimicrobials, vaccines, and diagnostics to combat escalating antimicrobial resistance.
